items.foco: Leukocoria as a cardinal sign of retinoblastoma
items.title: Leukocoria as a cardinal sign
items.contentHtml:
Leukocoria as a cardinal sign

  • The leukocoria (white pupillary reflex) is the cardinal sign of retinoblastoma and appears when the tumor occupies more than 50% of the vitreous or detaches the retina.
  • It is typically detected in flash photos (asymmetric white reflex) or in the examination with direct ophthalmoscope.
  • Leukocoria is not pathognomonic; Its differential diagnosis includes Coats' disease, persistence of hyperplastic primary vitreous, congenital cataract, retinopathy of prematurity and ocular toxocariasis.
  • The presence of intratumoral calcifications on ocular ultrasound strongly suggests retinoblastoma.

items.foco: Typical age of presentation before 5 years
items.title: Age at presentation <5 years
items.contentHtml:
Typical age of presentation before 5 years old

  • Retinoblastoma is the most common intraocular tumor in childhood; 90% of cases are diagnosed before the age of 5 years.
  • The hereditary form occurs earlier (average age 12-18 months), while the sporadic form is usually diagnosed between 2 and 3 years.
  • It is exceptional after 5 years; When diagnosed at an older age, reconsider alternative diagnoses such as Coats' disease or uveal melanoma.
  • The age of presentation is key in clinical suspicion: every child <5 years old with leukocoria or strabismus should be evaluated to rule out retinoblastoma.

items.foco: Mutation of the RB1 gene on chromosome 13q14
items.title: RB1 mutation at 13q14
items.contentHtml:
Mutation of the RB1 gene on chromosome 13q14

  • The RB1 gene is located at 13q14 and encodes the protein pRb, a key tumor suppressor in the control of the cell cycle (G1/S phase).
  • Biallelic inactivation of RB1 (model Knudson, two hits) is necessary for the development of retinoblastoma.
  • In the hereditary form there is a germline mutation (first hit) present in all cells, and a second somatic hit in the retina; In the sporadic case, both hits are somatic in the same retinal cell.
  • The mutation of RB1 confers a predisposition to second primary tumor (osteosarcoma, soft tissue sarcoma, melanoma) in patients with hereditary retinoblastoma.

items.title: bilateral hereditary form
items.contentHtml:
Bilateral and multifocal hereditary form

  • Hereditary retinoblastoma represents 40% of cases and is characterized by being bilateral and multifocal (multiple tumors in one or both eyes).
  • It is inherited in an autosomal dominant manner with 90% penetrance; 25% of cases have a family history, 75% are de novo mutations.
  • The age of diagnosis is younger (average 12 months) and is associated with a higher risk of second primary tumor (osteosarcoma, sarcomas, melanoma) due to the RB1 germline mutation.
  • Conservative treatment (systemic chemotherapy, thermotherapy, cryotherapy) is a priority to preserve vision, but requires lifelong oncological surveillance.

items.title: Unilateral sporadic form
items.contentHtml:
Unilateral and unifocal sporadic form

  • Sporadic retinoblastoma constitutes 60% of cases, is unilateral and typically unifocal (a single tumor).
  • Both mutations of the RB1 gene are somatic, occurring in a single retinal cell; There is no germline mutation or risk of hereditary transmission.
  • The average age of presentation is 24-36 months; It is diagnosed later than the hereditary form.
  • The treatment of choice for large or advanced tumors is enucleation, although small tumors can be managed with laser photocoagulation or cryotherapy.

items.foco: Classic triad: leukocoria, strabismus, red eye
items.title: classical triad
items.contentHtml:
Classic triad: leukocoria, strabismus, red eye

  • The classic triad of retinoblastoma includes leukocoria, strabismus and red eye; Although it is diagnosed earlier today, it is still useful in late presentations.
  • Leukocoria is the white pupillary reflex (most common sign, 60% of cases).
  • Strabismus (generally esotropia) occurs due to loss of central visual fixation when the tumor affects the macula.
  • Red eye and inflammation (pseudouveitis) can appear due to secondary glaucoma or tumor necrosis; Its presence suggests advanced disease.
  • Other symptoms: nystagmus, poor vision, proptosis, hyphema (blood in the anterior chamber).

items.title: Differential diagnosis: with Coats disease
items.contentHtml:
Differential diagnosis with Coats disease

  • Coats disease is the main imitator of retinoblastoma; It is characterized by retinal telangiectasias with massive subretinal lipid exudation that produces leukocoria.
  • Unlike retinoblastoma, Coats' disease is unilateral (90%), more common in men (3:1) and does not present intratumoral calcifications on ultrasound.
  • Fluorescein angiography shows telangiectasias with late leakage in Coats, while in retinoblastoma there are anomalous tumor vessels.
  • The age at presentation of Coats is somewhat older (5-10 years) vs retinoblastoma (<5 years).

items.title: Calcifications on ultrasound
items.contentHtml:
Ocular ultrasound shows intratumoral calcifications

  • B-mode ocular ultrasound is the initial imaging test; The presence of intratumoral calcifications with posterior acoustic shadowing is highly suggestive of retinoblastoma.
  • Calcifications are observed as hyperechoic foci with shadow artifact; they are detectable in >90% of retinoblastomas.
  • Orbital computed tomography shows calcifications with greater sensitivity, but is avoided by radiation in children; magnetic resonance imaging without contrast is preferred to evaluate extraocular extension and optic nerve.
  • The absence of calcifications on ultrasound should raise suspicion of alternative diagnoses such as Coats' disease, persistence of hyperplastic primary vitreous or toxocariasis.

items.foco: Treatment according to staging: enucleation vs chemotherapy
items.title: Treatment: enucleation vs chemotherapy
items.contentHtml:
Treatment according to staging: enucleation vs chemotherapy

  • Enucleation is the treatment of choice for large tumors (groups D/E of the International Classification of Retinoblastoma) or when there is no potential for useful vision.
  • Systemic chemotherapy (carboplatin, etoposide, vincristine) is used for tumor reduction before therapy focal (chemoreduction) in group B/C tumors.
  • Intra-arterial chemotherapy (melphalan) has gained ground for advanced unilateral tumors, achieving high control rates without enucleation.
  • External radiotherapy is reserved for refractory cases due to the risk of hereditary second tumors.

items.title: Laser for small tumors
items.contentHtml:
Laser photocoagulation for small tumors

  • laser photocoagulation (argon or diode laser) is indicated for small tumors (<3 mm base and <2 mm thickness), without vitreous or subretinal extension.
  • It is applied in 2-3 sessions, coagulating the tumor tissue and its vasculature; the response is evaluated by disappearance of the tumor and formation of a chorioretinal scar.
  • Cryotherapy is an alternative for peripheral tumors <3 mm, applying triple freezing-thawing.
  • Both techniques are focal therapies and are used alone or after systemic chemoreduction; require strict follow-up to detect recurrence.

items.foco: Evaluation of second primary tumor in hereditary form
items.title: Second primary hereditary tumor
items.contentHtml:
Evaluation of second primary tumor in hereditary form

  • Patients with hereditary retinoblastoma (germline mutation RB1) are at high risk of second primary tumor, especially osteosarcoma (femur, skull), soft tissue sarcoma and melanoma.
  • The risk increases with prior external radiation therapy; That is why it is avoided in the hereditary form.
  • Follow-up includes annual clinical evaluation for life, with imaging studies directed according to symptoms (bone x-ray, soft tissue magnetic resonance imaging).
  • genetic counseling is mandatory for the family; Siblings and children should be evaluated by ophthalmology from birth.

items.title: Screening in family members
items.contentHtml:
Ophthalmological screening in first-degree relatives

  • The first-degree relatives (parents, siblings) of a child with retinoblastoma should undergo ophthalmological screening under pupillary dilation from birth.
  • If an RB1 germline mutation is identified in the patient, relatives should be genetically evaluated; If they are carriers, they require serial ophthalmological examinations every 4-6 weeks until the age of 3 years, then spaced apart.
  • In neonates of parents with hereditary retinoblastoma, the first ophthalmological examination should be performed in the first week of life, under anesthesia if necessary.
  • Early screening allows tumors to be detected in early stages, increasing the possibilities of conservative treatment and visual preservation.
